title: Multithreading Basics

seo.title: Multithreading in Java - Thread, Runnable, Synchronization, Deadlock | Modern Age Coders
seo.description: Learn Java multithreading including Thread class, Runnable interface, thread lifecycle, synchronization, deadlock, volatile keyword, inter-thread communication, and Executor framework. Includes 58+ practice questions with interview-focused content.
seo.keywords: java multithreading, java thread, synchronized java, runnable vs thread java, java thread lifecycle, java deadlock, java volatile, java thread pool, java concurrency, java inter-thread communication

content.what:
What Is Multithreading?

A thread is the smallest unit of execution within a process. A Java program starts with one thread -- the main thread. Multithreading means running multiple threads concurrently within the same program, allowing different parts of your code to execute simultaneously.

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("Current thread: " + Thread.currentThread().getName());
        // Output: Current thread: main
    }
}

Thread vs Process

A process is an independent program running in its own memory space. A thread is a lightweight unit of execution within a process. Key differences:

  • Memory: Processes have separate memory spaces. Threads within the same process share the same heap memory but have their own stack.
  • Creation cost: Creating a thread is cheaper than creating a process.
  • Communication: Threads can communicate directly through shared memory. Processes need inter-process communication (IPC) mechanisms.
  • Isolation: A crash in one process does not affect others. A crash in one thread can bring down the entire process.

When Arjun's web server handles 100 users simultaneously, it does not create 100 separate processes. It creates 100 threads within one process, sharing the same application code and data.
content.why:
Why Is Multithreading Important?

1. Better Utilization of CPU Resources

Modern CPUs have multiple cores. A single-threaded program uses only one core while the rest sit idle. Multithreading allows your program to use all available cores. If Priya's data processing application runs 4 threads on a 4-core CPU, it can process data up to 4 times faster than a single-threaded version.

2. Responsive User Interfaces

In a GUI application, if a heavy computation runs on the main thread, the interface freezes. By running the computation on a separate thread, the UI remains responsive. This is why Android mandates that network calls happen on background threads.

3. Server-Side Scalability

Web servers use threads to handle multiple client requests simultaneously. When Vikram's Spring Boot application receives 1000 requests per second, each request is handled by a separate thread from a thread pool. Without multithreading, the server would process requests one at a time.

4. Real-World Applications

Multithreading is everywhere: web servers (Tomcat uses thread pools), databases (concurrent query processing), games (rendering + physics + AI on separate threads), download managers (parallel file downloads), and IDEs (code compilation while you type).

5. Interview and Placement Significance

Multithreading is one of the most asked topics in Java interviews at companies like Amazon, Flipkart, Goldman Sachs, and Morgan Stanley. Questions on thread lifecycle, synchronization, deadlock, and the volatile keyword appear regularly.
content.explanation:
Detailed Explanation

1. Creating Threads: Extending the Thread Class

The simplest way to create a thread is to extend the Thread class and override its run() method:

class MyThread extends Thread {
    @Override
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
            System.out.println(getName() + ": count " + i);
            try {
                Thread.sleep(500); // pause for 500ms
            } catch (InterruptedException e) {
                System.out.println("Thread interrupted");
            }
        }
    }
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        MyThread t1 = new MyThread();
        MyThread t2 = new MyThread();
        t1.setName("Thread-A");
        t2.setName("Thread-B");
        t1.start(); // creates a new thread and calls run()
        t2.start();
        System.out.println("Main thread continues...");
    }
}

Critical point: You must call start(), not run(). Calling run() directly executes the method on the current thread (no new thread is created). start() creates a new thread and then invokes run() on that new thread.

2. Creating Threads: Implementing Runnable

The preferred approach is to implement the Runnable interface:

class MyTask implements Runnable {
    private String taskName;

    public MyTask(String name) {
        this.taskName = name;
    }

    @Override
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
            System.out.println(taskName + ": step " + i);
        }
    }
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Thread t1 = new Thread(new MyTask("Download"));
        Thread t2 = new Thread(new MyTask("Upload"));
        t1.start();
        t2.start();
    }
}

Why prefer Runnable over extending Thread? (1) Java allows only single inheritance -- if you extend Thread, you cannot extend another class. (2) Runnable separates the task from the thread mechanism, following better design. (3) Runnable tasks can be submitted to thread pools (Executor framework).

3. Thread Lifecycle

A thread goes through five states during its lifetime:

NEW  ->  RUNNABLE  ->  RUNNING  ->  TERMINATED
                         |    ^  
                         v    |
                       BLOCKED/WAITING
  • NEW: Thread object created but start() not yet called.
  • RUNNABLE: After start() is called, the thread is ready to run but waiting for CPU time.
  • RUNNING: The thread scheduler has assigned CPU time and the thread is executing.
  • BLOCKED/WAITING: The thread is alive but not eligible to run. This happens when waiting for a lock (BLOCKED), calling wait() (WAITING), or calling sleep()/join() with timeout (TIMED_WAITING).
  • TERMINATED: The run() method has completed (or an unhandled exception occurred). The thread cannot be restarted.

4. Important Thread Methods

Thread t = new Thread(() -> {
    System.out.println("Running: " + Thread.currentThread().getName());
});

// Before start
t.setName("Worker-1");           // Set thread name
t.setPriority(Thread.MAX_PRIORITY); // 1 to 10 (default 5)
t.setDaemon(true);               // Daemon thread (ends when main ends)

t.start();                        // Start the thread

// Thread info
System.out.println(t.getName());  // Worker-1
System.out.println(t.isAlive());  // true if not terminated
System.out.println(t.getState()); // NEW, RUNNABLE, etc.

// Static methods (affect current thread)
Thread.sleep(1000);               // Pause current thread for 1 second
Thread.currentThread();           // Get reference to current thread
Thread.yield();                   // Hint to scheduler to give other threads a turn

5. join() -- Waiting for a Thread to Finish

Thread t = new Thread(() -> {
    try {
        Thread.sleep(2000);
        System.out.println("Thread finished");
    } catch (InterruptedException e) {
        e.printStackTrace();
    }
});

t.start();
System.out.println("Main waiting...");
t.join(); // Main thread blocks here until t finishes
System.out.println("Main continues after thread finished");

join() makes the calling thread wait until the target thread terminates. This is essential when you need the result of a thread before proceeding. Without join(), the main thread would continue immediately without waiting.

6. Synchronization

When multiple threads access shared data, race conditions can occur. Synchronization ensures that only one thread accesses a critical section at a time:

class BankAccount {
    private int balance = 1000;

    // Synchronized method -- only one thread can execute this at a time
    public synchronized void withdraw(int amount) {
        if (balance >= amount) {
            System.out.println(Thread.currentThread().getName() 
                + " withdrawing " + amount);
            balance -= amount;
            System.out.println("Remaining balance: " + balance);
        } else {
            System.out.println(Thread.currentThread().getName() 
                + ": insufficient funds");
        }
    }

    public synchronized int getBalance() {
        return balance;
    }
}

Without synchronized, two threads could both check balance >= amount simultaneously, both see sufficient funds, and both withdraw -- resulting in a negative balance. The synchronized keyword acquires a lock on the object, allowing only one thread to execute any synchronized method on that object at a time.

Synchronized Blocks

Instead of synchronizing the entire method, you can synchronize only the critical section:

public void withdraw(int amount) {
    // Non-critical code can run concurrently
    System.out.println("Processing request...");

    synchronized (this) {
        // Only this block is locked
        if (balance >= amount) {
            balance -= amount;
        }
    }
}

Synchronized blocks are preferred when only a small part of the method needs protection. They reduce the time a thread holds a lock, improving concurrency.

7. The volatile Keyword

volatile ensures that reads and writes to a variable go directly to main memory, not a thread-local cache:

class StopFlag {
    private volatile boolean running = true;

    public void stop() {
        running = false; // Immediately visible to all threads
    }

    public void run() {
        while (running) {
            // Do work
        }
        System.out.println("Stopped.");
    }
}

Without volatile, a thread might cache the value of running and never see the update from another thread, causing an infinite loop. volatile guarantees visibility but does NOT guarantee atomicity -- it is not a replacement for synchronized when you need compound operations (check-then-act).

8. Deadlock

A deadlock occurs when two or more threads are blocked forever, each waiting for a lock that the other holds:

Object lockA = new Object();
Object lockB = new Object();

// Thread 1: locks A, then tries to lock B
Thread t1 = new Thread(() -> {
    synchronized (lockA) {
        System.out.println("T1: holding lockA");
        try { Thread.sleep(100); } catch (InterruptedException e) {}
        synchronized (lockB) {
            System.out.println("T1: holding lockA and lockB");
        }
    }
});

// Thread 2: locks B, then tries to lock A
Thread t2 = new Thread(() -> {
    synchronized (lockB) {
        System.out.println("T2: holding lockB");
        try { Thread.sleep(100); } catch (InterruptedException e) {}
        synchronized (lockA) {
            System.out.println("T2: holding lockB and lockA");
        }
    }
});

t1.start();
t2.start();
// DEADLOCK: T1 holds A, waits for B. T2 holds B, waits for A.

How to avoid deadlocks: (1) Always acquire locks in the same order. (2) Use timeouts with tryLock() from java.util.concurrent.locks. (3) Minimize the scope and duration of locks. (4) Avoid nested locks when possible.

9. Inter-Thread Communication: wait(), notify(), notifyAll()

Threads can communicate using wait(), notify(), and notifyAll(). These methods must be called from within a synchronized block:

class SharedBuffer {
    private int data;
    private boolean hasData = false;

    public synchronized void produce(int value) throws InterruptedException {
        while (hasData) {
            wait(); // Release lock and wait until consumed
        }
        data = value;
        hasData = true;
        System.out.println("Produced: " + value);
        notify(); // Wake up waiting consumer
    }

    public synchronized int consume() throws InterruptedException {
        while (!hasData) {
            wait(); // Release lock and wait until produced
        }
        hasData = false;
        System.out.println("Consumed: " + data);
        notify(); // Wake up waiting producer
        return data;
    }
}

wait() releases the lock and puts the thread to sleep until another thread calls notify() on the same object. notify() wakes up one waiting thread. notifyAll() wakes up all waiting threads. Always use while (not if) before wait() to guard against spurious wakeups.

10. Executor Framework

Creating threads manually is inefficient for large-scale applications. The Executor framework provides thread pools:

import java.util.concurrent.ExecutorService;
import java.util.concurrent.Executors;

public class ExecutorDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Create a pool of 3 threads
        ExecutorService executor = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(3);

        // Submit 5 tasks to the pool
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            int taskId = i;
            executor.submit(() -> {
                System.out.println("Task " + taskId 
                    + " running on " + Thread.currentThread().getName());
                try { Thread.sleep(1000); } catch (InterruptedException e) {}
            });
        }

        executor.shutdown(); // No new tasks, finish existing ones
        System.out.println("All tasks submitted.");
    }
}

A thread pool reuses a fixed number of threads to execute multiple tasks. When a thread finishes one task, it picks up the next from the queue. This avoids the overhead of creating and destroying threads repeatedly. shutdown() initiates an orderly shutdown -- no new tasks are accepted, but existing tasks complete.

codeExamples.title: Creating Threads: Thread Class vs Runnable Interface
codeExamples.explanation: Three ways to create threads are shown. Extending Thread is simplest but prevents extending other classes. Implementing Runnable is preferred because it preserves inheritance flexibility. Lambda syntax (Java 8+) is the most concise, since Runnable is a functional interface with a single abstract method.
codeExamples.output:
ThreadClass: 1
ThreadClass: 2
ThreadClass: 3
Runnable: 1
Runnable: 2
Runnable: 3
Lambda: 1
Lambda: 2
Lambda: 3
(Output order may vary due to thread scheduling)

codeExamples.title: Thread Lifecycle and join()
codeExamples.explanation: getState() returns the thread's current lifecycle state. After creation, the state is NEW. After start(), it becomes RUNNABLE. During Thread.sleep(), it becomes TIMED_WAITING. After completion, it is TERMINATED. join() makes the main thread wait until the worker thread finishes.
codeExamples.output:
State after creation: NEW
State after start: RUNNABLE
State during sleep: TIMED_WAITING
Main: waiting for worker...
Worker: started
Worker: finished
State after join: TERMINATED
Main: worker is done, continuing.

codeExamples.title: Race Condition and Synchronization Fix
codeExamples.explanation: The count++ operation is not atomic -- it involves read, increment, and write. Without synchronized, two threads can read the same value simultaneously and both write back the same incremented value, losing one increment. The synchronized keyword ensures only one thread executes increment() at a time.

codeExamples.title: Deadlock Demonstration
codeExamples.explanation: This is a classic deadlock. Thread-1 acquires LOCK_A and then tries to acquire LOCK_B. Thread-2 acquires LOCK_B and then tries to acquire LOCK_A. Each thread holds the lock the other needs, and neither can release its lock (because it is waiting inside a synchronized block). The program hangs indefinitely.
codeExamples.output:
Thread-1: acquired LOCK_A
Thread-2: acquired LOCK_B
Thread-1: waiting for LOCK_B...
Thread-2: waiting for LOCK_A...
(Program hangs -- deadlock)

codeExamples.title: Producer-Consumer with wait/notify
codeExamples.explanation: This implements the producer-consumer pattern using wait() and notify(). The producer waits if the buffer is full (hasMessage == true). The consumer waits if the buffer is empty. wait() releases the lock and suspends the thread. notify() wakes up one waiting thread. The while loop around wait() guards against spurious wakeups.
codeExamples.output:
Produced: Hello
Consumed: Hello
Produced: World
Consumed: World
Produced: Java
Consumed: Java
Produced: Done
Consumed: Done

codeExamples.title: ExecutorService Thread Pool
codeExamples.explanation: A FixedThreadPool with 3 threads handles 6 tasks. The first 3 tasks start immediately (one per thread). The remaining 3 wait in a queue until a thread becomes available. shutdown() stops accepting new tasks. awaitTermination() blocks until all tasks finish or the timeout expires. Thread pools are far more efficient than creating individual threads.
codeExamples.output:
Task 1 started on pool-1-thread-1
Task 2 started on pool-1-thread-2
Task 3 started on pool-1-thread-3
Task 1 completed on pool-1-thread-1
Task 4 started on pool-1-thread-1
Task 2 completed on pool-1-thread-2
Task 5 started on pool-1-thread-2
Task 3 completed on pool-1-thread-3
Task 6 started on pool-1-thread-3
Task 4 completed on pool-1-thread-1
Task 5 completed on pool-1-thread-2
Task 6 completed on pool-1-thread-3
All tasks finished: true

codeExamples.title: volatile Keyword: Visibility Guarantee
codeExamples.explanation: Without volatile, the worker thread might cache the value of running in its CPU cache and never see the update from the main thread, causing an infinite loop. volatile forces every read to go to main memory, guaranteeing that the worker thread sees the change immediately. This is the most common use case for volatile: a stop flag.
codeExamples.output:
Main: set running to false
Worker stopped after 4527831 iterations.
Main: worker has stopped.

commonMistakes.title: Calling run() Instead of start()
commonMistakes.error: Output: Running on: main (Expected a new thread, but code runs on the main thread.)
commonMistakes.explanation: Calling run() directly is just a normal method call on the current thread. It does not create a new thread. You must call start(), which allocates a new thread and then calls run() on that thread.

commonMistakes.title: Not Synchronizing Shared Data
commonMistakes.error: Race condition: count++ is not atomic (read-modify-write). Two threads may read the same value and overwrite each other's increment.
commonMistakes.explanation: The count++ operation involves three steps: read count, add 1, write count. Without synchronized, two threads can interleave these steps, losing increments. The synchronized keyword ensures only one thread executes the method at a time.

commonMistakes.title: Using if Instead of while Before wait()
commonMistakes.error: Spurious wakeup: the thread may wake up even though the condition is still false, leading to incorrect behavior.
commonMistakes.explanation: Java does not guarantee that a thread wakes up only when notify() is called. Spurious wakeups can happen. Using while re-checks the condition after every wakeup, ensuring the thread only proceeds when the condition is actually true.

commonMistakes.title: Calling wait()/notify() Outside a Synchronized Block
commonMistakes.wrongCode:
Object lock = new Object();
lock.wait(); // IllegalMonitorStateException!
commonMistakes.error: IllegalMonitorStateException: current thread is not the owner of the object's monitor.
commonMistakes.explanation: wait(), notify(), and notifyAll() must be called from within a synchronized block on the same object. The thread must own the object's monitor (lock) before calling these methods. Without the lock, Java throws IllegalMonitorStateException.

commonMistakes.title: Not Handling InterruptedException
commonMistakes.wrongCode: Thread.sleep(1000); // Compile error in non-throwing method
commonMistakes.error: Compile error: unreported exception InterruptedException; must be caught or declared to be thrown.
commonMistakes.explanation: InterruptedException is a checked exception thrown by sleep(), join(), and wait(). You must catch it or declare it. Best practice is to catch it and call Thread.currentThread().interrupt() to preserve the interrupt status for higher-level handlers.

summary: A thread is the smallest unit of execution. A process can have multiple threads sharing the same heap memory but with separate stacks. Creating threads is cheaper than creating processes.

Two ways to create threads: extend Thread class (override run()) or implement Runnable interface (preferred because Java allows only single inheritance). Lambda syntax works since Runnable is a functional interface.
Call start() to create a new thread. Never call run() directly -- that executes the method on the current thread without creating a new one.

Thread lifecycle: NEW (created) -> RUNNABLE (start called) -> RUNNING (executing) -> BLOCKED/WAITING (waiting for lock or signal) -> TERMINATED (run completed). A terminated thread cannot be restarted.

Key thread methods: sleep(ms) pauses the current thread, join() waits for another thread to finish, isAlive() checks if a thread is running, setName()/getName() manage thread identity, setPriority() hints at scheduling priority (1-10).
synchronized keyword prevents race conditions by allowing only one thread to execute a critical section at a time. Use synchronized methods or synchronized blocks (blocks are more granular).
volatile keyword guarantees that reads and writes go to main memory, not thread-local cache. It provides visibility but not atomicity. Use it for simple flags, not compound operations.
Deadlock occurs when two or more threads each hold a lock and wait for a lock held by the other. Avoid by always acquiring locks in the same order and minimizing lock scope.
wait(), notify(), and notifyAll() enable inter-thread communication. They must be called inside a synchronized block. Always use while (not if) before wait() to guard against spurious wakeups.
The Executor framework (ExecutorService, Executors.newFixedThreadPool) provides thread pools that reuse threads for multiple tasks, avoiding the overhead of creating threads repeatedly.

topicSpecific.hint: Every Java program starts with a main thread.
topicSpecific.explanation: Every Java program starts execution on the main thread. Thread.currentThread() returns the currently executing thread, and getName() returns its name, which is "main" by default.

topicSpecific.hint: run() is called directly, not start().
topicSpecific.explanation: Calling run() directly does NOT create a new thread. It executes the lambda on the main thread. So "Hello" prints first, then "World". The output is deterministic because everything runs on one thread.

topicSpecific.hint: Check the thread state before and after calling start().
topicSpecific.answer:
State: NEW
State: RUNNABLE
Background
(Output order of last two lines may vary)
topicSpecific.explanation: Before start(), the thread is in NEW state. After start(), it transitions to RUNNABLE. The "Background" output may appear before or after the second state print, depending on thread scheduling.

topicSpecific.hint: setName() changes the thread's name before it starts.
topicSpecific.explanation: setName("Worker") sets the thread's name. When the thread runs, Thread.currentThread().getName() returns "Worker".

topicSpecific.hint: join() makes the main thread wait for t to finish.
topicSpecific.explanation: join() blocks the main thread until thread t finishes. So all three "A" prints complete before "B" is printed. The output order is guaranteed.

topicSpecific.hint: interrupt() wakes a sleeping thread by throwing InterruptedException.
topicSpecific.explanation: The thread starts sleeping for 2 seconds. interrupt() immediately wakes it by throwing InterruptedException. The catch block prints "Interrupted!", then execution continues to print "Done".

topicSpecific.hint: The start() method is overridden. What does the overridden version do?
topicSpecific.explanation: The start() method is overridden to just print "start" without calling super.start(). Since super.start() is not called, no new thread is created and run() is never invoked. Only "start" is printed.

topicSpecific.hint: Can a thread be started more than once?
topicSpecific.answer:
Hello
Then IllegalThreadStateException is thrown on the second start() call.
topicSpecific.explanation: A thread can only be started once. The first start() succeeds and the thread runs. The second start() throws IllegalThreadStateException because the thread is already started (or terminated). A terminated thread cannot be restarted.

topicSpecific.hint: Can a thread re-enter a synchronized method on the same object?
topicSpecific.explanation: Java's synchronized locks are reentrant. A thread that already holds the lock on an object can enter another synchronized method on the same object without deadlocking. The thread already owns the monitor, so it proceeds.

topicSpecific.hint: Both threads synchronize on the same lock (Main.class). T1 acquires it first.
topicSpecific.explanation: T1 starts first and acquires the lock on Main.class. T2 starts and blocks waiting for the lock. After T1 finishes and releases the lock, T2 acquires it and prints "T2 locked". The output order is guaranteed because of the synchronization.

topicSpecific.question: What is the difference between sleep() and wait()?
topicSpecific.hint: Think about lock behavior and where each can be called.
topicSpecific.answer: sleep() pauses the current thread for a specified time and does NOT release any lock it holds. It can be called anywhere. wait() releases the lock on the object and waits until notify() or notifyAll() is called. It must be called inside a synchronized block. sleep() is a static method of Thread, while wait() is an instance method of Object.
topicSpecific.explanation: Key distinction: sleep holds the lock (other threads still cannot enter synchronized blocks), wait releases the lock (allowing other threads to acquire it and potentially call notify).

topicSpecific.question: Why should you prefer implementing Runnable over extending Thread?
topicSpecific.hint: Think about Java's inheritance model and the Executor framework.
topicSpecific.answer: Three reasons: (1) Java supports only single inheritance. If you extend Thread, you cannot extend any other class. Implementing Runnable preserves your inheritance slot. (2) Runnable separates the task (what to do) from the thread mechanism (how to run it), following better OOP design. (3) Runnable objects can be submitted to ExecutorService thread pools, enabling thread reuse and better resource management.
topicSpecific.explanation: In practice, extending Thread is only used in simple examples. Production code uses Runnable (or Callable for tasks that return a result) with the Executor framework.

topicSpecific.question: What is the difference between synchronized and volatile?
topicSpecific.answer: synchronized provides both mutual exclusion (only one thread executes the critical section) and visibility (changes are flushed to main memory). volatile provides only visibility (reads/writes go to main memory) but NOT mutual exclusion. Use synchronized for compound operations (check-then-act, read-modify-write). Use volatile for simple flags that are written by one thread and read by others.
topicSpecific.explanation: Example: count++ requires synchronized because it is read-modify-write. A boolean running flag that is set to false by one thread can use volatile because it is a simple write.

topicSpecific.question: What are four conditions required for a deadlock to occur (Coffman conditions)?
topicSpecific.hint: All four must be present simultaneously for deadlock.
topicSpecific.answer: (1) Mutual Exclusion: At least one resource is held in a non-sharable mode. (2) Hold and Wait: A thread holds at least one resource and waits for another. (3) No Preemption: Resources cannot be forcibly taken from a thread; it must release them voluntarily. (4) Circular Wait: A circular chain of threads exists, each waiting for a resource held by the next. Eliminating any one condition prevents deadlock.
topicSpecific.explanation: The most practical prevention strategy is eliminating Circular Wait by always acquiring locks in a consistent global order. If all threads acquire LOCK_A before LOCK_B, circular wait cannot occur.

mixed.hint: isAlive() returns true if the thread has been started and has not yet terminated.
mixed.answer:
false
true
Go
(The last two lines may appear in either order)
mixed.explanation: Before start(), isAlive() returns false (thread is in NEW state). After start(), the thread is alive. The "Go" output and the second isAlive check may interleave.

mixed.hint: Daemon threads are terminated when all non-daemon threads finish.
mixed.answer:
Main done
("Running" may or may not appear)
mixed.explanation: Daemon threads are background threads. The JVM exits when all non-daemon threads (here, just the main thread) finish. Since the main thread completes almost immediately after starting the daemon thread, the daemon thread may not get a chance to print.

mixed.hint: join() after t1.start() means t1 finishes before t2 starts.
mixed.explanation: t1.join() after t1.start() makes the main thread wait for t1 to finish. So all three A's print first. Then t2 starts and all three B's print. The order is guaranteed because of the join calls.

method1() is synchronized, so the first thread holds the lock while sleeping. But method2() is NOT synchronized, so the second thread can execute it freely without needing the lock. "method2" prints during method1's sleep.

mixed.hint: A single-thread executor runs tasks sequentially.
mixed.explanation: newSingleThreadExecutor() creates a pool with one thread. Tasks are executed sequentially in the order they were submitted. The output order is guaranteed.

mixed.hint: setPriority() sets the thread's priority before it starts.
mixed.explanation: setPriority(8) sets the thread's priority to 8 (range is 1-10, default is 5). Inside the thread, getPriority() returns 8. Note: thread priority is only a hint to the scheduler and does not guarantee execution order.

mixed.hint: add() is synchronized. Is there a race condition?
mixed.explanation: The add() method is synchronized, so only one thread can execute it at a time. Each thread adds 5000, and there is no race condition. The result is always 10000.

mixed.hint: T1 calls wait(), T2 calls notify() after 500ms.
mixed.explanation: T1 acquires the lock and calls wait(), which releases the lock and suspends T1. T2 sleeps 500ms, then acquires the lock and calls notify(), waking T1. After T2 exits the synchronized block, T1 reacquires the lock and prints "T1 resumed".

mixed.question: What is the difference between a thread and a process?
mixed.hint: Think about memory sharing and weight.
mixed.answer: A process is an independent program with its own memory space. A thread is a lightweight unit of execution within a process. Threads within the same process share heap memory but have separate stacks. Creating a thread is cheaper than creating a process. Threads communicate through shared memory; processes need IPC mechanisms.
mixed.explanation: A Java program runs as one process. Calling new Thread().start() creates a new thread within that process, sharing the same heap. Using Runtime.exec() would create a new process with its own memory.

mixed.question: What is a thread pool, and why is it better than creating threads manually?
mixed.hint: Think about the cost of creating and destroying threads.
mixed.answer: A thread pool maintains a fixed number of reusable threads. Tasks are submitted to a queue, and available threads pick them up. Benefits: (1) Avoids the overhead of creating/destroying threads for each task. (2) Controls the maximum number of concurrent threads, preventing resource exhaustion. (3) Provides task queuing when all threads are busy. (4) Simplifies thread management code. Java's ExecutorService (e.g., Executors.newFixedThreadPool()) provides this.
mixed.explanation: If a web server creates a new thread per request and gets 10,000 requests, it creates 10,000 threads, consuming massive memory. A thread pool with 200 threads handles the same load by queuing excess requests.

mcqs.question: Which method must you call to start a new thread in Java?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. start() creates a new thread and calls run() on it. Calling run() directly executes the method on the current thread.

mcqs.question: What interface should you implement to define a thread's task?
mcqs.explanation: A is correct. Runnable is the standard interface for defining a thread's task. Callable is also valid (it can return a value), but Runnable is the basic one.

mcqs.question: What is the default name of the thread that runs the main() method?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. The main thread is named "main". Threads created with new Thread() are named "Thread-0", "Thread-1", etc. by default.

mcqs.question: What does Thread.sleep(1000) do?
options: Creates a thread that runs for 1 second
Pauses the current thread for 1000 milliseconds
Terminates the current thread after 1 second
Waits for another thread for 1 second
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Thread.sleep(1000) pauses the currently executing thread for 1000 milliseconds (1 second). It does not release any locks held.

mcqs.question: Can a terminated thread be restarted?
options: No, a terminated thread cannot be restarted
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. A terminated thread cannot be restarted. Calling start() on a terminated thread throws IllegalThreadStateException. You must create a new Thread object.

mcqs.question: What does the synchronized keyword do?
options: Ensures only one thread can execute the method/block at a time on the same object
Prevents the method from throwing exceptions
Makes the method run on a separate thread
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. synchronized acquires a lock on the object, ensuring mutual exclusion. Only one thread can execute synchronized methods/blocks on the same object at a time.

mcqs.question: What exception does Thread.sleep() throw?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Thread.sleep() throws InterruptedException if another thread interrupts the sleeping thread. It is a checked exception that must be handled.

mcqs.question: What is a daemon thread?
options: A thread with the highest priority
A background thread that is terminated when all non-daemon threads finish
A thread that cannot be interrupted
A thread that runs only once
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Daemon threads are background threads (e.g., garbage collector). The JVM exits when only daemon threads remain. Set with thread.setDaemon(true) before start().

mcqs.question: What is the purpose of the join() method?
options: To combine two threads into one
To make the calling thread wait until the target thread finishes
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. t.join() makes the calling thread (usually main) block until thread t terminates. It is used when you need the result of a thread before proceeding.

mcqs.question: What does volatile ensure?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. volatile ensures that reads and writes go to main memory, not a thread-local cache. It guarantees visibility but NOT atomicity or mutual exclusion.

mcqs.question: What happens if you call wait() outside a synchronized block?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. wait() must be called while holding the object's monitor (inside a synchronized block). Calling it without the lock throws IllegalMonitorStateException at runtime (not a compile error).

mcqs.question: What is the difference between notify() and notifyAll()?
options: notify() wakes all threads; notifyAll() wakes one
notify() wakes one waiting thread; notifyAll() wakes all waiting threads
notify() is for threads; notifyAll() is for processes
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. notify() wakes up one arbitrary thread waiting on the object's monitor. notifyAll() wakes up all waiting threads, and they compete for the lock. Use notifyAll() when multiple threads might be waiting for different conditions.

mcqs.question: Which is NOT a way to prevent deadlock?
options: Always acquire locks in the same order
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. Increasing thread priority does not prevent deadlock. Deadlock is about the order and duration of lock acquisition, not thread priority. Options A, B, and D are all valid deadlock prevention strategies.

mcqs.question: What does Executors.newFixedThreadPool(5) create?
options: A pool of 5 reusable threads that process tasks from a queue
A thread that runs 5 tasks sequentially
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. A fixed thread pool maintains 5 threads that pick up tasks from a shared queue. If more than 5 tasks are submitted, excess tasks wait in the queue until a thread becomes available. Threads are reused, not destroyed after each task.

mcqs.question: What is the range of thread priorities in Java?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Thread priorities range from 1 (MIN_PRIORITY) to 10 (MAX_PRIORITY), with 5 (NORM_PRIORITY) as the default. Priority is a hint to the scheduler and does not guarantee execution order.

mcqs.question: Why should you use while (not if) before wait()?
options: To guard against spurious wakeups and re-check the condition
wait() only works inside while loops
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Spurious wakeups can cause a thread to wake up even without notify(). Using while re-checks the condition after every wakeup, ensuring the thread only proceeds when the condition is truly met.

codingChallenges.title: Thread-Safe Counter
codingChallenges.description: Create a thread-safe Counter class with increment(), decrement(), and getCount() methods. Test it with two threads incrementing 10000 times each and verify the final count is 20000.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use synchronized methods. Use join() to wait for threads before checking the count.

codingChallenges.title: Parallel Array Sum
codingChallenges.description: Split an array into two halves and compute the sum of each half in a separate thread. Then combine the results on the main thread. Compare with single-threaded sum.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use Runnable. Store each thread's result in a shared array or use a wrapper class.

codingChallenges.title: Producer-Consumer with Buffer
codingChallenges.description: Implement a bounded buffer (capacity 5) with produce() and consume() methods using wait/notify. The producer should produce 10 items, and the consumer should consume all 10.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use synchronized, wait(), and notify(). Use a LinkedList as the buffer.

codingChallenges.title: Thread Pool Task Processor
codingChallenges.description: Use ExecutorService with a fixed thread pool of 3 threads to process 8 tasks. Each task should print its ID and the thread executing it, then simulate work with Thread.sleep(). Print when all tasks are complete.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput:
Task 1 on pool-1-thread-1
Task 2 on pool-1-thread-2
... (all 8 tasks)
All tasks completed.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use Executors.newFixedThreadPool(). Use shutdown() and awaitTermination().

codingChallenges.title: Deadlock Detector and Fix
codingChallenges.description: First, write code that creates a deadlock between two threads. Then fix it by using a consistent lock ordering strategy. Print messages to show the execution flow in both versions.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput:
=== DEADLOCK VERSION ===
T1: locked A
T2: locked B
(hangs)

=== FIXED VERSION ===
T1: locked A
T1: locked B
T1: done
T2: locked A
T2: locked B
T2: done
codingChallenges.constraints: Demonstrate the deadlock scenario. Fix by acquiring locks in the same order in both threads.
